title: Does every 2-colored cocktail party graph admit a cover by two monochromatic diameter-2 subsets?
question: In every red-blue coloring of the cocktail party graph on an even number of vertices, can the vertex set be covered by two monochromatic diameter-2 subsets?
family_name: Low-diameter monochromatic covers in edge-colored graphs
canonical_source: Andras Gyarfas and Gabor N. Sarkozy, "2-Reachable Subsets in Two-Colored Graphs" (Graphs and Combinatorics 42, 2026), which states and relaxes the English-McCourt-Mattes-Phillips conjecture.
canonical_statement: In every 2-coloring of the edges of the cocktail party graph G^c, there exist A,B covering V(G^c) such that A and B are monochromatic diameter-2 subsets.
intended_statement: Upgrade the 2026 relaxed 2-reachable cover theorem to the original diameter-2 conjecture, or exhibit a minimal colored cocktail-party counterexample.
definitions: The cocktail party graph is obtained from a complete graph on an even number of vertices by deleting a perfect matching.
A diameter-2 subset in color i is a vertex set whose induced color-i subgraph has distance at most 2 between every pair of its vertices.
The 2026 source proves the relaxed version where the distance-2 witness may pass through vertices outside the subset.
attack_style: tighten the 2-reachable argument to internal diameter 2 via critical-pair analysis and blow-up-of-C5 obstruction control
theorem_slice_hint: Treat the exact cocktail-party conjecture as the main theorem, not as a stepping stone to full f(2,2)=3 for all alpha=2 graphs.
publication_red_flags: The gap between external 2-reachability and internal diameter 2 can hide subtle obstruction configurations.
A negative answer would need a very explicit coloring family, not an isolated small example.
publication_if_solved: Solving the original diameter-2 cocktail-party conjecture would read as a direct short follow-up to the 2026 relaxed theorem.
paper_shape: One conjecture-resolution note: statement, structural proof or counterexample, and a short discussion of Ryser-style diameter bounds.
pre_solve_gate_reason: The 2026 source already isolates the exact open conjecture and proves a nearby relaxation, so one strong solve would already be most of a standalone paper.
why_reasoning_friendly: The host graph is rigid, the color structure is binary, and the gap from 2-reachable to diameter 2 is conceptually narrow rather than campaign-sized.
why_low_token: The object definition is short, certificates are simple vertex covers in two colors, and the conjecture has a tight yes-or-no endpoint.
verifier_hint: A positive witness is a pair of color classes with direct internal diameter checks; a negative result needs a fully specified 2-coloring family with provable failure of every two-set cover.
lean_hint: If solved positively, only formalize the fixed graph family and diameter-2 cover predicate after the combinatorial proof stabilizes.
why_still_appears_open: The canonical 2026 paper states the diameter-2 cocktail-party conjecture explicitly, proves only the relaxed 2-reachable version, and the bounded recent-status search surfaced no later diameter-2 resolution.
why_this_could_be_publishable: It is a direct upgrade from a very recent paper's main relaxed theorem to the original exact conjecture.
attempted_conflict_check: The repo memory sweep found no prior queue item, selected problem, failed slug, or campaign near-duplicate involving cocktail-party diameter-2 monochromatic covers.

title: Does the ladder with 4 rungs have the edge-Erdos-Posa property?
question: If H is the ladder with 4 rungs, must every graph either contain k edge-disjoint H-subdivisions or admit an edge set of size f(k) hitting all H-subdivisions?
family_name: Edge-Erdos-Posa property for fixed planar graphs
named_conjecture: 4-rung ladder edge-Erdos-Posa test case after the Steck-Ulmer 3-rung / 14-rung split
canonical_source: Raphael Steck and Arthur Ulmer, "On the Edge-Erdos-Posa Property of Ladders" (Graphs and Combinatorics 40, 2024).
canonical_statement: Determine whether the ladder with 4 rungs has the edge-Erdos-Posa property.
intended_statement: Settle the smallest unresolved ladder case by proving that the 4-rung ladder has the edge-Erdos-Posa property, or by constructing a genuine counterexample graph not ruled out by the condensed-wall barrier.
definitions: A ladder with t rungs is the graph formed by two parallel paths with t matching edges between corresponding vertices.

A graph H has the edge-Erdos-Posa property if there is a function f such that every graph G either contains k edge-disjoint subdivisions of H or an edge set of size at most f(k) meeting every subdivision of H.
The 2024 source proves the property for the 3-rung ladder and shows that ladders with 14 or more rungs do not have it.
attack_style: extend the 3-rung positive proof and isolate the first extra obstruction pattern, or build the first non-condensed-wall counterexample
theorem_slice_hint: Treat the 4-rung ladder as the smallest unresolved rung count, not as a broad ladder campaign; the whole note should pivot on one yes-or-no theorem.
publication_red_flags: A negative answer would need a clean counterexample mechanism, not just an ad hoc gadget.
If the positive proof merely replays the 3-rung argument with long case splits, packaging quality could slip.
publication_if_solved: A resolution of the 4-rung ladder case is already a standalone note because the 2024 paper leaves ladders with 4 to 13 rungs explicitly open.
paper_shape: A smallest-open-case theorem or counterexample note for a fixed pattern H, with one structural section and one consequence section.
pre_solve_gate_reason: This is already the smallest unresolved exact ladder case left open in the canonical 2024 source, so one clean resolution is itself most of a paper with no feeder ladder.
why_reasoning_friendly: The target graph H is tiny, the positive and negative sides are both crisp, and the canonical source already isolates the ladder-range gap rather than a sprawling family program.
why_low_token: The statement is short, certificates are compact, and the proof surface is concentrated on one fixed pattern instead of a large parameter family.
verifier_hint: For a positive solve, verify the hitting/packing theorem against the exact 4-rung ladder definition; for a negative solve, preserve an explicit host graph family together with a checked obstruction argument.
lean_hint: Lean only after the combinatorial core is stable; formalization would mostly be finite graph bookkeeping around the exact ladder pattern and edge-hit definition.
why_still_appears_open: The canonical 2024 paper explicitly asks whether ladders with 4 to 13 rungs have the edge-Erdos-Posa property, and the bounded later-status search surfaced no follow-up resolution for the 4-rung case.
why_this_could_be_publishable: It is a smallest-open-case yes/no theorem with immediate context from the 3-rung positive result and the 14-rung negative barrier.
attempted_conflict_check: The exclusion sweep over failed_problems.json, queue.json, selected_problem.md, ledger.md, PROOFS.md, campaigns/, campaign manifests, and artifact slugs found no prior AutoMath attempt or near-duplicate around ladder edge-Erdos-Posa cases.

title: Is every 9-regular graph on 12 vertices one-factorizable?
question: Does every 9-regular graph of order 12 admit a decomposition into 9 perfect matchings?
named_conjecture: Open degree-9 slice in the order-12 one-factorization classification
canonical_source: Petteri Kaski and Patric R. J. Ostergard, "One-Factorizations of Regular Graphs of Order 12" (Electronic Journal of Combinatorics 12, 2005).
canonical_statement: Classify one-factorizations of regular graphs of order 12; after the 2005 study, the unresolved degrees are 7, 8, and 9.
intended_statement: Settle the smallest high-degree unresolved slice by classifying the nine nonisomorphic 9-regular graphs on 12 vertices with respect to one-factorizability, ideally proving all are one-factorizable or isolating minimal exceptions.
definitions: A one-factor is a perfect matching.
A one-factorization of a k-regular graph is a partition of its edge set into k one-factors.
The 2005 source leaves the degree-7, degree-8, and degree-9 order-12 cases open and records that there are only 9 nonisomorphic 9-regular graphs on 12 vertices.
attack_style: near-complete-graph case split using perfect-matching structure, complement heuristics, and tiny exact verification on the nine degree-9 graphs
theorem_slice_hint: Do not chase the whole order-12 classification at once; isolate the degree-9 slice because it has only nine base graphs and already looks paper-shaped on its own.
publication_red_flags: The canonical open-status source is old, so any eventual verify stage must recheck for a quiet later classification.
A purely brute-force enumeration without a readable structural summary would weaken the packaging.
publication_if_solved: A complete degree-9 classification on order 12 is already a short exact-classification paper because the source explicitly leaves that slice open.
paper_shape: A fixed-order exact classification note with a table of the nine graphs, factorization verdicts, and a short structural discussion.
pre_solve_gate_reason: The degree-9 slice is already a self-contained classification target with only nine underlying graphs, so one successful classification is already most of a paper.
why_reasoning_friendly: The host set is finite and tiny, the certificate is a matching decomposition, and the degree-9 slice is cleaner than the broader unresolved order-12 program.
why_low_token: There are only nine nonisomorphic degree-9 graphs to certify, and each certificate is a list of perfect matchings with straightforward checking.
verifier_hint: For a positive solve, store each factorization explicitly as nine perfect matchings; for a negative solve, preserve the complete obstruction search or structural proof for every one of the nine graphs.
lean_hint: If a short structural proof emerges, formalization is feasible; if the result is purely computational, Lean should stay out of the critical path.
why_still_appears_open: The canonical 2005 paper explicitly leaves degrees 7, 8, and 9 open for order 12, and the bounded later-status search surfaced no later paper resolving the degree-9 slice.
why_this_could_be_publishable: It is an exact unresolved finite classification with a tiny verifier and essentially zero post-solve packaging tax.
attempted_conflict_check: The repo memory sweep found no earlier AutoMath attempt or near-duplicate on order-12 one-factorizations or degree-9 regular graph factorizations.

title: Does every 8-regular graph admit an F-avoiding orientation for every 3-element forbidden set?
question: For every 8-regular graph G and every forbidden set F subseteq {0,...,8} with |F|=3, must G admit an orientation whose vertex out-degrees avoid F?
canonical_source: Owen Henderschedt and Jessica McDonald, "On orientations with forbidden out-degrees" (Discrete Applied Mathematics 387, 2026).
canonical_statement: If G is d-regular and F subseteq {0,...,d} with |F| < d/2, then G admits an F-avoiding orientation.
intended_statement: Close the exact d=8 slice left after the 2026 advance by proving that every 8-regular graph avoids every 3-element forbidden list, or by finding a minimal 8-regular counterexample.
definitions: An F-avoiding orientation is an orientation in which each vertex has out-degree not belonging to F.
The 2026 source proves the conjecture for d <= 6 and, more generally, proves that every d-regular graph with d >= 5 avoids every forbidden list of size at most 2.
For d = 8 the conjectural threshold |F| < d/2 means exactly the new unresolved case |F| = 3.
attack_style: compress the 3-element forbidden-list patterns using complement symmetry and extend the lasso-based local repair method from the 2026 paper
theorem_slice_hint: Package d=8 as a complete next even-degree theorem, with the list patterns normalized before any serious proof search.
publication_red_flags: The literature is very recent, so a later preprint could change the status quickly.
Some 3-element list patterns may resist the existing lasso and end-interval tools and force new local surgery.
publication_if_solved: A clean d=8 theorem or counterexample would already be a short follow-up paper to the 2026 d<=6 result.
paper_shape: A next-slice theorem paper: reduce to the distinct 3-element list types, prove the slice, and note the conjectural context.
pre_solve_gate_reason: The d=8, |F|=3 slice is a bounded next-case theorem with no feeder ladder, and solving it would already be close to a standalone paper rather than a long campaign.
why_reasoning_friendly: The degree is fixed, the list size is fixed, and the 2026 source contributes both a near-by theorem and a concrete local-repair toolkit.
why_low_token: The statement is compact, certificates are orientations with degree counts, and symmetry collapses the forbidden-list universe sharply.
verifier_hint: Positive certificates are explicit orientations checked by out-degree counts; negative results need a fully specified 8-regular witness plus a proof that all orientations hit F somewhere.
lean_hint: Formalization should wait for a final structural proof; afterward it is mostly finite orientation bookkeeping on a fixed-degree slice.
why_still_appears_open: The 2026 paper proves d <= 6 and all |F| <= 2 cases, which leaves d=8 with |F|=3 as a precise unresolved slice, and bounded later web search did not reveal a d=8 resolution.
why_this_could_be_publishable: It is the exact next even-degree slice after a fresh 2026 advance, with a ready-made paper narrative and tiny verification footprint.
attempted_conflict_check: No matching slug, title, or near-duplicate forbidden-outdegree d=8 slice appeared in the repo exclusion sweep; only the distinct d=7 slice is currently selected elsewhere in repo memory.

title: Are all graphs on 9 vertices 1-11-representable?
question: Does every graph with 9 vertices admit a 1-11 representation, or is there a minimal 9-vertex obstruction?
canonical_source: Mohammed Alshammari, Sergey Kitaev, Chaoliang Tang, Tianyi Tao, and Junchi Zhang, "On 1-11-representability and multi-1-11-representability of graphs" (Utilitas Mathematica 122, 2025), together with Futorny-Kitaev-Pyatkin, "New Tools to Study 1-11-Representation of Graphs" (Graphs and Combinatorics 40, 2024).
canonical_statement: It is unknown whether every graph is 1-11-representable; the 2025 paper proves all graphs on at most 8 vertices are 1-11-representable and explicitly points toward the 9-vertex slice.
intended_statement: Resolve the first unresolved finite slice by proving that every 9-vertex graph is 1-11-representable, or by producing the first 9-vertex counterexample with a rigorous certificate.
definitions: A graph is 1-11-representable if there is a word whose two-letter subwords witness edges by allowing at most one pair of consecutive equal letters and non-edges by forcing at least two.
Word-representable graphs are exactly the 0-11-representable graphs.
The 2025 source proves all graphs on at most 8 vertices are 1-11-representable and notes the 9-vertex case as the next natural finite target.
attack_style: use the 2024-2025 structural toolbox first, then only bounded exhaustive search on the remaining 9-vertex non-word-representable cases
theorem_slice_hint: Target the 9-vertex universal statement or the first obstruction directly; do not drift into the full all-graphs conjecture.
publication_red_flags: This can drift into a brute-force census if the structural reductions are not used aggressively.
If all 9-vertex graphs are representable, the paper still needs a concise certificate strategy rather than a raw machine dump.
publication_if_solved: Settling the 9-vertex slice would already be a finite-classification note, either as a complete positive census or as a first minimal counterexample paper.
paper_shape: A smallest-unresolved finite-slice note with structural reductions, a compact classification table, and explicit representing words or a minimal obstruction.
pre_solve_gate_reason: The 9-vertex slice is already a self-contained smallest unresolved finite classification, so a full settlement would itself be most of a paper without any feeder ladder.
why_reasoning_friendly: The problem has a rich reduction toolbox, a sharply bounded finite frontier, and a natural minimal-counterexample flavor.
why_low_token: The theorem statement is tiny, witnesses are explicit words, and the remaining frontier is a finite census rather than an open-ended family.
verifier_hint: Positive solutions should preserve explicit 1-11 words for each reduced isomorphism class or a general construction; negative solutions need a complete obstruction certificate for the claimed 9-vertex graph.
lean_hint: Only formalize after a human-readable certificate style is fixed; the right backend object is a checker for two-letter subword constraints, not a large search proof.
why_still_appears_open: The 2025 paper still states that it is unknown whether every graph is 1-11-representable, proves only the up-to-8-vertex slice, and its concluding discussion explicitly identifies the 9-vertex frontier as the next natural target; bounded web search found no later 9-vertex resolution.
why_this_could_be_publishable: It is exactly the kind of smallest unresolved finite slice that can become a clean note or a minimal-counterexample paper with low novelty-check cost.
attempted_conflict_check: The repo exclusion sweep found no existing AutoMath artifact, queue entry, or failed problem near the 1-11-representability 9-vertex slice.
